coredata.dc:title: Specific locations of blue and green-emitting units in dual emissive carbon dots and their reversible emitting properties due to switchable inter-chromophoric interactions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Bhuyan, Rahul
Bramhaiah, Kommula
Bhattacharyya, Santanu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are the unique class of luminescent nanomaterials consist of various chromophoric units heterogeneously distributed throughout the nanoparticle, resulting intriguing multistate emissive properties. Herein, we have critically investigated the specific locations of the blue and green-emitting centers inside dual emissive CDs by steady-state and time-resolved polarized emission study. It is further clarified by a temperature-dependent fluorescence study for both the emitting domains. Results suggest that the blue chromophoric units are located at the interior part of CDs, while green units are mostly at the exterior region. Furthermore, we have investigated the solvent-dependent inter-chromophoric interactions between the two emissive domains by the Time-Resolved Area Normalized Emission Spectroscopy (TRANES). Results suggest that at polar aprotic solvent acetone, time-dependent positive evolution of green-emitting states and negative evolution of blue emissive domains have been observed. This reversible emitting properties evolve due to the excited state energy migration from blue emissive domains to green emissive domains at polar aprotic medium, while in the case of polar protic solvent water, this phenomenon is missing. This switchable inter-chromophoric interaction are correlated further with the inter-particle interactions of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual emissive Carbon dots
Polarized emission
Time-resolved area normalized emission spectroscopy
And Thermoresponsive photoluminescence

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of n doped carbon dots (N-CDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of CDs synthesis employing pyrolysis approach (b) absorption spectra of CD2; inset shows the absorption spectra of CD1 and CD3 (c) fluorescence emission spectra of CD2 at various excitation wavelengths such as λex-340 nm and λex-405 nm.

(a) time-resolved fluorescence anisotropy data of CD2 in water (b) ethylene glycol (c) glycerol solvents, and (d) Schematic illustration of the plausible location of the fluorophores moieties such as blue-emitting and green-emitting states.

Thermoresponsive properties of CD2 synthesized employing pyrolysis approach (a) PL spectra of CD2 excitation at 340 nm (b) PL spectra of CD2 excitation at 405 nm (c) plot of Ft/F0 vs. temperature (cooling and heating process).

Steady-state anisotropy plot for the blue and green emissive domains vs. temperature (heating and cooling process); (a) bar diagram of initial anisotropy (r0) of different emissive domains at room temperature (b) temperature-dependent anisotropy plot for blue emissive domains and (c) temperature-dependent anisotropy plot for green emissive domains.

(a) absorption spectra of CD2 in the water and acetone solvents (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CD2 in the water and acetone solvents (c) TRANES of CD2 in the water medium (d) TRANES of CD2 in the acetone medium (e) Schematic illustration of energy transfer from blue emissive dot to the green emissive carbon dot with the excitation of blue domain with λex 340 nm.
(a) HRTEM image of CD2 in water medium: inset displays the lattice spacings (b) HRTEM of the CD2 in an aggregated form in acetone medium.

(a) The reversibility of the aggregation and de-aggregation of CDs in acetone and water solvent mixtures; systematically changing the volume of acetone solvent (b) schematic illustration of aggregation and de-aggregation of CD2 by changing the water to acetone ratio.
Average time-resolved anisotropic data along with a hydrodynamic size of blue and green emissive domains in various viscous solvents.

head.ce:title: Specific locations of blue and green-emitting units in dual emissive carbon dots and their reversible emitting properties due to switchable inter-chromophoric interactions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Indian Institute of Science Education and Research, Govt. ITI Building (transit campus), Engg. School Road, Berhampur, Odisha 760010 India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Indian Institute of Science Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Indian Institute of Science Education and Research, Govt. ITI Building (transit campus), Engg. School Road, Berhampur, Odisha 760010 India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are the unique class of luminescent nanomaterials consist of various chromophoric units heterogeneously distributed throughout the nanoparticle, resulting intriguing multistate emissive properties. Herein, we have critically investigated the specific locations of the blue and green-emitting centers inside dual emissive CDs by steady-state and time-resolved polarized emission study. It is further clarified by a temperature-dependent fluorescence study for both the emitting domains. Results suggest that the blue chromophoric units are located at the interior part of CDs, while green units are mostly at the exterior region. Furthermore, we have investigated the solvent-dependent inter-chromophoric interactions between the two emissive domains by the Time-Resolved Area Normalized Emission Spectroscopy (TRANES). Results suggest that at polar aprotic solvent acetone, time-dependent positive evolution of green-emitting states and negative evolution of blue emissive domains have been observed. This reversible emitting properties evolve due to the excited state energy migration from blue emissive domains to green emissive domains at polar aprotic medium, while in the case of polar protic solvent water, this phenomenon is missing. This switchable inter-chromophoric interaction are correlated further with the inter-particle interactions of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have gained extensive attention due to their non-toxic nature, bio-compatibility, low-cost, chemical inertness, and ease of preparation along with interesting photoluminescence (PL) properties.[1–3] Typically, CDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles comprising of both amorphous and crystalline graphitic domains and various surface functional groups/defects.[1–4] In general, CDs show both excitation-dependent and/or independent PL properties with large stokes shift (∼100 nm) between the absorption and emission peak maxima. The unique PL properties of CDs alter by varying the synthesis parameters, reactant's nature, and their concentrations, various hetero atom incorporation, etc.[5–7] Considering the intriguing properties of CDs, this new class of materials is feasible in various applications including energy harvesting, optronics, chemical, and biological sensing, catalysis, and light-emitting devices.[2,8–13] However, the intrinsic features of CDs are too much complicated, and fundamental structure–property correlation is still debatable.[14] Till date, the majority of the reports explained the excitation-dependent emission properties in terms of surface states while the graphitic core remains almost non-emissive.[15,16] However, the presence of polycyclic aromatic molecules and the formation of self-trapped excitons can support the excitation-dependent emission properties of CDs without considering surface states.[17,18] Furthermore, the existence of highly emissive small molecular fluorophores is also another important counterpart to be considered.[19,20] Overall, the multistate emissive domains of CDs are too much complicated to generalize.[4,21,22] Although the intriguing emission properties of CDs are mainly dominated by three major constituent moieties, i.e.-i) highly defined aromatic domains (also larger polycyclic aromatic molecules), ii) emissive surface states originating from several functional groups and surface-defects, iii) Existence of small molecular fluorophores.[17,23] Among all the major constituents, small molecular fluorophores can be attached with the CDs or can stay as a separate by-product during the carbonization process.[1,24] However, the precise locations of the small fluorophore molecules are still not clear. While free, it can show higher rotational motion and enhanced nonradiative decay rates. On the other hand, it can show different rotational behavior while attached on the surface or completely confined inside the nanoparticles.[25,26] The different locations of small molecular fluorophores can change the overall photophysical processes as well as overall photoluminescence properties. Therefore, it is essential to find out the specific locations of small molecular chromophoric units and their interchromophoric interactions to optimize CDs as efficient emitting materials.

Recently Das et al. have synthesized carbon dots by using citric acid and triethanolamine as precursors employing the reflux approach. To know the location of the fluorophore moiety, they performed the rotational anisotropy decay dynamics and resonance energy transfer (RET) dynamics. From the rotational correlation time and RET dynamics, they concluded that the fluorescence moiety residing inside the flexible core environment of CDs.[27] Depending on the precursor, they have observed only blue emissive CDs; i.e.- mainly one type of chromophoric unit. However, the existence of several types of chromophoric units inside CDs is very much obvious depending on the precursors and synthesis parameters. Therefore, understanding the specific locations of different types of chromophoric centers and their interchromophoric interactions is very much crucial. Considering the existence of multichromophoric moieties and several by-products, systematic separation techniques (like electrophoresis, chromatography etc.) are also required. It is noteworthy that, solvent polarity controls the surface states and interchromophoric interactions which eventually vary the ensemble average photoluminescence properties.[28]

Herein, we have synthesized CDs by using citric acid and urea as carbon and nitrogen precursors respectively by using the simple pyrolysis method. The as-synthesized CDs show both blue and green emissive domains. The ratiometric proportion of blue and the green emissive domains can be controlled by changing the ratio of citric acid and urea.[24,29,30] Besides, previous reports have already mentioned the molecular origin of blue and green-emitting states for citric acid and urea-based CDs.[24] In the literature, previous reports are also there, explaining the solvent-dependent tunable PL emissions of CDs. [31,32] However, molecular insight into the solvent dependent multistate emissive properties are not well explored. In addition, investigation of specific locations for multiple emitting units and insight into their complex interplay for switchable PL properties are extremely challenging for CDs. Therefore, after careful separation of both the emitting centers by column chromatography, we have performed time-resolved fluorescence anisotropy to understand the rotational motion of the blue and green-emitting molecules, respectively. Results suggest that blue chromophoric centers are embedded inside the more rigid interior domain of CDs, while green-emitting chromophores are located inside the less rigid exterior domain of CDs. Furthermore, completely different temperature-dependent PL properties have been observed for both the blue and green chromophoric units, respectively. It clearly suggests the different environments for these two different emitting centers. Steady-state polarized emission further supports our investigations. Finally, solvent-dependent switchable PL properties based on aggregation-assisted interchromophoric interactions have been identified by steady-state PL and time-resolved area normalized emission spectroscopy (TRANES). Results suggest a clear indication of interchromophoric exciton energy migration from the blue chromophoric center to the green one. For further clarification, detailed fluorescence properties have been explored by varying the ratiometric proportion of blue and green-emitting centers. Overall, the current investigation will fundamentally lead us to the detailed insight of multistate emissive CDs and their further optimization for tunable emitting devices.

Citric acid anhydrous (<99%), urea (<99%), were purchased from Himedia Pvt. Ltd. Ethylene glycol anhydrous, (<99.8%), cyclohexanol (<99%), and glycerol (≥99.0%) purchased from Sigma Aldrich. The chemicals were used without any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of n doped carbon dots (N-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, 1 g of citric acid anhydrous along with 10 mL of water was taken in a glass beaker followed by the addition of 2 g urea. The whole reaction mixture was pyrolyzed at 250 °C under ambient conditions. At the initial stage, the colorless liquid turned to an orange-yellow colored homogenous solution. At the end of the reaction, the whole solvent was allowed to vaporized under open conditions, and the final solid product turned to reddish-black precipitate (sticky liquid). Finally, the formed sticky liquid was re-dispersed in milli-Q water for further characterizations. We have varying amounts of urea content (0.1 g, 2 g, and 4 g, respectively) at a fixed concentration of citric acid (1 g) and the respectively CDs are named as CD1, CD2, and CD3 respectively.

Absorption spectra of the as-synthesized CDs were measured using a Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer. Steady-state fluorescence emission measurements, PL-based thermo-responsive measurements, and steady-state anisotropy measurements were recorded employing Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog-3, equipped with a 300 W xenon lamp as the excitation source and Peltier cooling setup for the temperature controller (heating and cooling cycles). The morphology and crystallinity of the as-synthesized CDs were imaged using a TALOS F200S G2 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. For this, the diluted samples of CDs were dropped cast over the holey carbon-coated copper grids, and dried in a vacuum. Time-resolved fluorescence lifetime measurements were carried using a Fluorescence spectrometer LifeSpec II (EDINBURGH Instruments) with 340 nm (resolution ∼ 80 ps) UV-LEDs (EPLED Series) and 405 nm (resolution ∼ 20 ps) LASER light as the excitation source. For anisotropy decays, we used a motorized polarizer in the emission side. The analyzer was rotated by 90° C at regular intervals.

One of the facile and cheapest routes to synthesize dual emissive CDs from the citric acid and urea are simple bottom-up based thermal decomposition at ambient condition (depicted in Fig. 1 a).[33] It is already reported before that during the synthesis, the citric acid and urea decompose, dehydrated, and reduced at higher temperatures and gives the bright luminescent materials. Initially, at a lower temperature, the eutectic mixture of citric acid and urea is connected by an H-bonding network caused by the amphiphilic H-bond properties of both components. With increasing the reaction temperature, the urea dissociates into isocyanic acid and ammonia, followed by condensation with citric acid, and forms a citrazinic acid as an initial molecular fluorophore.[29,34] These preformed citrazinic acid molecular fluorophores further react with the isocyanic acid and form the 4-hydroxy-1Hpyrrolo[3,4-c] pyridine-1,3,6(2H,5H)-trione (HPPT).[24,29,35]

As mentioned before, we have varied the relative extent of both blue and green-emitting chromophoric units in CDs by simply varying the urea content at a fixed amount of citric acid, i.e.- CD1, CD2, and CD3 respectively. CD1 and CD3 show mostly blue and green emission respectively, while in case of CD2, both the blue and green emissive domains are equally predominant. To evaluate further, we have measured the absorption and emission spectra of all the as-synthesized CDs (depicted in Fig. 1 b & c and fig. S1). The UV–visible absorption spectrum of CD2 displays a typical band around 270 nm corresponding to the π-π* transitions of sp2 hybridized CC carbons. In addition, a couple of strong peaks are observed around 336 nm and 410 nm, which corresponds to the n-π* transitions corresponding to the nitrogen and oxygen containing groups originated specifically from blue-emitting and green-emitting fluorophore molecules as well as several functional groups heterogeneously distributed throughout the CDs. Furthermore, it is nicely matches with the previous reports.[14,33] The inset of Fig. 1b displays the absorption spectra of CD1 (blue emissive moiety) and CD3 (majority green emissive moiety), respectively. We don’t observe any specific absorption band at longer wavelength region (>400 nm) for CD1, which further suggests that the green-emitting centers are absent for CD1. On the other hand, in the case of CD3, a strong and intense peak is observed around 410 nm, including one low intense peak around 333 nm which suggests that relative populations of green chromophoric centers are more compare to blue in CD3. Further, the emission features of the as-synthesized CDs are obtained by photoluminescence measurements. The excitation-dependent PL emission spectra of CD1, CD2, and CD3 are depicted in figure S1. In general, all the CDs show excitation-dependent PL spectra. However, in case of CD1, emission maxima concentrate mainly in the blue region (∼452 nm). In contrast to that, CD3 mainly shows an excitation-dependent PL emission property with a peak maxima around ∼ 520 nm at the excitation of 410 nm. Traces amount of blue emission at ∼ 452 nm was also observed for the CD3. However, in case of CD2, both blue and green emissive domains are concentrated.

For further clarification regarding the interchromophoric interactions and their contribution to the overall photoluminescence properties, it is extremely important to know the specific locations of these two different emissive centers. Time-dependent fluorescence anisotropy of the chromophoric centers can unveil information about the location of these specific chromophoric units at their respective excitation wavelength.[27] In general, fluorescence anisotropy measures the rotational mobility of the fluorophore moieties that are excited with polarized light. It is well known that fluorescence anisotropy measurements offer information regarding the shape, size, location of the fluorescence moiety, and characteristics of surrounding environments.[25] The time-resolved anisotropies of different emissive states are calculated by using the following equation.[36]
(1)
r t
=
I ‖
t
- G
I ⊥
t
I ‖
t
+ 2 G
I ⊥
t
where G is the correction factor for the detector sensitivity to the polarization direction of the emission, which can be determined by exciting the sample with horizontally polarized light and detecting the intensity of the polarized light emitted horizontally and vertically. I∥ is the intensity of the detected light when the excitation and emission polarization in parallel and I⊥ is the intensity of the detected light when the excitation and emission polarization in perpendicular. Therefore, fluorescence anisotropy is the ratio of a difference between the emitted parallel and perpendicular polarized light to the total intensity of the polarized light emitted by the sample. Furthermore, the fluorescence anisotropy is dimensionless and independent of the concentration of the fluorophore material. In general, fluorescent nanoparticle with spherical nature normally shows complete depolarized emission as there is no direction of transition dipole moment. However, both blue and the green emissive domains of CDs show time-resolved polarized emission which directly suggests that the whole CDs are not fluorescence but it is location-specific. The possible locations of the chromophoric units in CDs can be as follows, (a) It can be inside the rigid/amorphous matrix of the CDs. In case of a rigid matrix the rotational correlation time should depend on the overall rotation of the nanoparticle, however, in case of an amorphous matrix we will have confined rotational motions of the chromophoric units and it will not depend on the overall rotation of the CDs. b) Secondly the chromophoric units can be separately attached on the CD surface by some covalent bond. In this particular case rotational motion of the chromophoric units also depends on the overall rotation of the CDs. Finally c) we can have completely isolated free chromophoric units. In the 3rd case, we will have normal rotational motions of the free chromophoric units in a solvent medium. Fig. 2 a shows the time-resolved polarized emission decay curves for both the blue and green emissive domains of CD2 at 340 nm and 405 nm wavelength of excitations, respectively. The r(0) values for blue and green emissive domains are 0.18 and 0.13, respectively. Both the anisotropy decay curves are single exponential with a rotational correlation time of 840 and 120 ps for blue and green parts respectively. However, the approximated anisotropy decay times will be<50 ps for free blue (citrazinic acid) and green (HPPT) molecules. Therefore, we can confirmly say that both the blue and green chromophoric units are inside the amorphous CD matrix. However, the initial anisotropy and average reorientation time of blue emissive domains are 7 times higher than the green emissive domains. Therefore, we can conclude that matrix rigidity for blue emissive chromophoric centers is higher than the green ones. In CDs, the interior domains are more rigid compare to the exterior surface domains. Blue emitting chromophoric centers are located inside the interior domain of CDs, while the green one is located at the exterior of the CDs. We have also calculated the possible hydrodynamic radius of blue and green chromophoric units by using the following equations.[36]
(2)
r
hy
=
3
k B
T
τ r
4 π η
3
where rhy, kB, T, τr, and η are hydrodynamic radium, Boltzmann constant, the temperature in absolute scale, rotational correlation time, and viscosity of the solvent, respectively. The calculated hydrodynamic diameter of the blue and green-emitting moieties, which are residing inside the CDs (size of the CDs from HRTEM is around ∼ 10 nm) are around 0.96 nm (size of the blue-emitting moiety) and 0.51 nm (size of the green-emitting moiety), respectively in an aqueous medium. For further clarification, we have varied the solvent medium with different viscosity (Fig. 2b &c). The average reorientation time of both the emissive domains gradually increases with increasing the viscosity of the solvent medium (as depicted in Table 1 ). Therefore, we can clearly say that both the chromophoric units are inside the amorphous domains of CDs where the solvent can enter, however, the blue one is inside the interior domain with more rigidity while the green one is inside the exterior domain with less rigidity. Now it is very important to understand whether both types of chromophoric units are inside the same CDs or inside the two different types of CDs. The locations of two different chromophoric moities are schematically depicted in fig. 2d. To clarify further we have carried out thin layer chromatography (TLC) and column chromatography. In TLC we have completely separated two distinguished spots which indicate two types of CDs with separate blue and green emissive domains respectively. Finally, we have fully separated blue emissive CDs and green emissive CDs by column chromatography (for CD2) and are depicted in figure S2. The detail UV–Vis absorption and steady state PL study has been carried out. We have observed comparable results with the CD1, CD2, and CD3 respectively (Fig.S3). Furthermore, the reaction mechanism can be speculated as follows (as depicted in fig. S4 and additional note with fig. S4 in supporting information). Initially, the citrazinic acid molecules (blue-emitting moiety) are formed by the condensation of citric acid and urea[29,30] followed by the development of the interior domain of CDs, resulting the formation of the blue-emitting units. During the course of further pyrolysis the initially formed citrazinic acid or its derivative molecules act as the seed molecules to form green emitting HPPT molecules after reacting with excess isocyanic acid (formed from the decomposition of urea) present in the reaction medium [24]. As a result, the green-emitting moiety has been formed motsly in the exterior part of the CDs.

A Thermoresponsive PL study has been carried out for both the emitting domains to get further clarification about their location (as depicted in Fig. 3 ). Temperature can indirectly affect the matrix rigidity/ flexibility of the surrounding environment for the chromophoric units, which eventually can change the overall PL properties. Fig. 3a & b show the temperature-dependent color-coded PL spectra of blue and green emissive domains of the CD2 at 340 nm and 405 nm excitation wavelengths respectively in the temperature range of 15 °C- 80 °C (heating and cooling process). The aqueous solutions of as-synthesized CDs are exposed for 2 min at each temperature interval (for stabilization) to observe the changes in the PL emission intensity. The observed results such as temperature vs. PL emission intensity demonstrates that the CDs can be utilized as temperature sensors. It is quite clear that the luminescence intensity of the blue CDs (λex 340 nm) gradually decreases with increasing temperature, and it can be recovered to the original state when the temperature falls back to its initial temperature, i.e.-15 °C. Conversely, while exciting the green emissive domains at 405 nm excitation wavelength, the PL emission intensity gradually increases with increasing the temperature and the PL intensity can be again recovered to its original state by decreasing the temperature to the initial state (15 °C). Moreover, over the measured temperature range, no significant changes in peak maxima, broadening, and peak area of PL emission spectra have been observed. For further clarification, we have plotted a relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of both the blue and green emissive domains as a function of temperature, and depicted in Fig. 3c. where F0 and F are the fluorescent intensity of the CDs at initial and elevated temperatures, respectively. The decrease (λex 340 nm) and increase (λex 405 nm) of PL intensities are 44.4 % and 13.4 %, respectively. The decrease in PL intensity corresponding to the blue emissive domains can be due to the increased randomness of the surrounding amorphous matrix which eventually increases the nonradiative relaxation process.[14] It will further support that the blue emissive domains are specifically embedded inside the amorphous domain of CDs. While in the case of 405 nm excitation, the PL intensity corresponding to the green emissive domains increases with temperature, which can be defined as the increased mobility of HPPT molecules and decrease of intermolecular interactions between similar types of molecules. It is further suggesting the existence of green chromophoric domains on the exterior domain of CDs. Additionally, we have measured the thermoresponsive properties for the blue and green emissive CDs, after separation through column chromatography, and are exhibiting comparable results with the temperature-dependent PL properties of the as synthesised CD2 (Fig.S5).

Along with the temperature-dependent, PL properties for both the emissive domains, steady-state polarized emission study by means of steady-state anisotropy, followed by temperature dependency (in between 14 °C and 80 °C; both reversible heating and cooling process) has also been carried out for further clarification (depicted in Fig. 4 ). In general, room temperature steady-state anisotropy can provide us subsidiary information about the surrounding environments experienced by both the blue and green emissive domains respectively. The blue emissive domains (λex = 340 nm; λem = 440 nm) exhibits an initial anisotropy value of r = 0.28, which suggests that the fluorophore moiety residing inside the more rigid environment of CDs, while in the case of a green-emitting domain, the initial anisotropy value was around r = 0.034 (Fig. 4a). Depolarization of fluorescence anisotropy for specific green emissive domains supports its location on the exterior domain of CDs which is exposed to the solvent. Further, it can be observed that with increasing the temperature both the emissive domains getting depolarized and recovered back while decreasing the temperature to the initial temperature (Fig. 4b & c). Similar depolarization supports the enhanced randomness of both the emitting domains. Furthermore, we have done the temperature-dependent steady-state anisotropy measurements for the blue and green emissive CDs after the separation employing Column chromatography and are exhibiting similar results (Fig.S6).

To understand the interchromophoric interactions between two different emissive domains, we have varied the solvent nature from water to acetone. Fig. 5 a displays the corresponding absorption spectra of CD2 in water and acetone medium respectively. By changing the solvent medium from water to acetone, no significant changes are observed in the absorption spectra (as depicted in Fig. 5a) which further suggests that there is no change in ground state interactions while changing the nature of the solvent. However, a clear change in steady-state fluorescence spectra has been observed (as depicted in Fig. 5b). In the water medium, the CD2 shows a broad emission peak with peak maxima around 440 nm (λex = 340 nm). While in the case of acetone medium, the blue emissive band is quenched, while the intensity of the green emissive domain increases. As we haven’t seen any change in absorption spectra, therefore it clearly suggests excited-state interactions between the two different types of chromophoric domains. It can be assigned as a nonradiative energy transfer process between the two chromophoric units. To confirm further, we have performed time-resolved area normalized emission spectra (TRANES) measurements. TRANES is commonly used to study the excited state dynamics and kinetics of the fluorescent molecules present in the solvent mediums. Furthermore, it can help us to elucidate the number of species contributing to the observed fluorescence emission at a specific region. TRANES spectra are obtained in four steps as described earlier.[37,38] (a) Firstly, the collection of fluorescence decays at 5 nm intervals in the entire range of emission spectra. (b) Fitting the as-collected fluorescence decay curves with the suitable exponential function and acquire the wavelength-dependent parameters like lifetime values and their amplitudes (c) construction of time-resolved emission spectra (TRES) employing steady-state fluorescence emission spectra (corrected for the photomultiplier response) (d) normalize the area of the TRES to a constant value (generally to the area of the spectrum at time t = 0 ns) to attain TRANES. From Fig. 5c, one can observe no noticeable changes in the emission spectra of CD2 in a water medium with the time evolution experiment from t = 0 ns to t = 23 ns, which confirms no possible interchromophoric interactions between the two emissive domains (see Figure S4a). While in the case of acetone medium, a bathochromic evolution of the fluorescence emission spectra from 416 nm to 480 nm is observed with the time evolution experiment from t = 0 ns to t = 23 ns (Fig. 5d & fig. S4b). It clearly confirms the exciton energy migration from blue emissive domains towards the green emissive domains due to the nonradiative FRET process. Further, we have calculated the radiative and non-radiative decay kinetics of CD2 by varying the water to acetone solvent medium. The values are as follows that the non-radiative rate increases from 1.3 × 108 s−1 to 2.93 × 108 s−1, whereas the radiative rate is almost constant (6.584 × 106 s−1). The calculated time for energy transfer from the blue to green CDs is around 6.15 ns. To get further insight into the energy transfer process by varying the relative populations of blue and the green emissive domains, we have conducted TRANES measurements for the CD1 (mostly blue emissive) and CD3 (mostly green emissive) systems (as depicted in figure S3 & S4). The CD1 displays mainly blue emission and no noticeable changes are observed with the time evolution experiment in acetone medium. While in the case of CD3, significant bathochromic temporal evolution has been observed from 412 nm to 480 nm (fig. S7 & S8). The initial emission at 412 nm corresponds to the blue emissive chromophoric units. Although, the relative population of the blue-emitting chromophore is significantly low. As time progresses, the green emission starts dominating at 480 nm. Therefore, we can conclude that excited state energy migration from blue chromophoric units to green chromophoric units predominately increases due to the nonradiative FRET process (schematically represented in Fig. 5e). Time-resolved fluorescence decay data further support that (as depicted in figure S7 and Table S1). After separation by column chromatography, pure blue emissive CDs display no noticeable temporal change in TRANES spectra (as depicted in fig. S9). Whereas, after mixing of pure green emitting CDs with blue one at various concentration ratios, we have observed a significant bathochromic temporal evolution from 412 nm to 480 nm, which suggest the similar exciton energy migration process from blue emitting domain to green one. However, the observed temporal evolution of TRANES is not that much prominent for the mixture of pure blue and green emissive CDs while comparing with the temporal evolution of CD2 (as mentioned before). This is due to the change of surface groups of the CDs during the purification process using column chromatography, which diminish the inter-domain interacations.

To correlate further the excited state photophysical behavior/ interchromophoric interactions with the morphological evolution through interparticle interactions, we have investigated the hydrodynamic radius of CD2 from the DLS study (as depicted in fig. S10) and also HR-TEM analysis both in water and acetone medium respectively (as depicted in Fig. 6 ). The calculated hydrodynamic radius of CD2 in the water medium is around 15 nm (depicted in fig. S10a), while it increases to 150–200 nm (depicted in fig. S10b) in the acetone medium. It confirms the increased interparticle interactions in the acetone medium. Furthermore, in an aqueous medium, the CD2 particles are spherical in size and uniformly distributed. The average particle size is around ∼ 10 nm. The inset of Fig. 6a shows a high-resolution HRTEM image with good crystalline nature along with a lattice fringe spacing of 0.34 nm, which is close to that of the graphite (002) plane.[14,39,40] However, the CD2 particles are aggregated and display a higher particle size around ∼ 100 nm in acetone medium (Fig. 6b). Although, the nature of the crystallinity and the crystal spacing remained unaltered both in aqueous and water medium. The TEM images of CD2 and CD3 have been shown in figure S11. Therefore, with decreasing the solvent polarity we can change the surface functionality of CDs which increases the interparticle interactions followed by enhanced interchromophoric interactions between the two emissive domains of CDs.

Further to know about the reversibility of these aggregations and de-aggregation of CDs by changing the solvent polarity (water to acetone), we have performed controlled experiments by changing the water to acetone solvent ratio and measured PL emission spectra (as depicted in Fig. 7 ). From Fig. 7a, one can clearly observe that the PL emission spectra of CDs in the water (CDs in 0% acetone) at 340 nm excitation wavelength show a broad emission spectrum with 435 nm emission maxima. As the acetone volume increases, the PL emission peak splits into two peaks with emission maxima of 435 nm and 490 nm. Further increasing the acetone volume, the PL emission peak around 490 nm becomes much more prominent. Finally, in the pure acetone solvent, the 490 nm peak was much intense than the 435 nm peak. The schematic illustration of the aggregation and de-aggregation of CD2 in water to acetone solvents was depicted in Fig. 7b. It further confirms that excited state interchromophoric interactions are completely reversible. It can be further utilized for tunable sensing and emitting devices.

In conclusion, we have synthesised dual emissive CDs from citric acid and urea as molecular precursors by using simple bottom-up based pyrolysis method. The locations of the different types of chromophoric units (both blue and green) are investigated by steady-state and time-resolved polarized emission study. Results suggest that blue chromophoric units are located inside the rigid interior domain of CDs, while green chromophoric units are located at the less rigid exterior domains. Temperature-dependent PL studies further clarify our investigations. Furthermore, interchromophoric interactions between different types of chromophoric units are investigated by TRANES study. In acetone medium, time-dependent positive evolution of green emissive domain has been observed, while the blue emissive domains are gradually quenched. This typical time-dependent photophysical interactions between the two different chromophoric domains suggest the excited state energy migration from blue emissive domain to the green emissive domain. However, this phenomenon is absent in aqeous medium. The solvent-dependent inter-chromophoric interactions are further supported by reversible aggregation and deaggreation behavior of CDs in acetone and aqueous medium respectively. Overall, this fundamental investigations will be beneficial for the optimization of of CDs as tunable emitting materials and their applications in photonics as well as optoelectronics devices.

Rahul Bhuyan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Kommula Bramhaiah: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Writing - original draft. Santanu Bhattacharyya: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: K.B. acknowledges IISER Berhampur for providing an institute postdoctoral fellowship. Authors acknowledge DST-SERB(grant numbers/2019/000026) for financial support and S. B. acknowledges IISER Berhampur for initiation grant(IG/21082018/B0035). The authors acknowledge Central Advanced Instrumentation Facility (CAIF) at IISER Berhampur. Authors acknowledge Dr. Neena S. John, CeNS, Bangalore for TEM measurements.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.07.119.
